subSlides.text: On the morning of October 22, 1907, the Knickerbocker Trust Company of New York suspended payments to depositors. Within hours, crowds gathered outside. The panic that followed nearly collapsed the entire American banking system before the Federal Reserve existed to stop it.

The crisis had roots in the San Francisco earthquake of 1906. Insurance claims drained gold from New York banks, tightening credit. Speculative schemes by copper magnate F. Augustus Heinze then ignited the fuse. When his attempt to corner the copper market collapsed, every bank connected to him came under suspicion.

Trust companies — lightly regulated institutions that held deposits but were not subject to reserve requirements — had expanded recklessly. They borrowed short and lent long into speculative ventures. When confidence broke, depositors ran. The trusts had no reserves to meet them.

In three weeks, the New York Stock Exchange fell fifty percent from its January peak. Call money rates — the overnight lending rate for brokers — spiked to one hundred fifty percent. Hundreds of banks across the country suspended payments. Business activity seized.

The United States had no central bank. There was no lender of last resort. What it had was J.P. Morgan — seventy years old, already a legend, and possessed of sufficient personal authority to do what no government institution could.

The structural problem the Panic of 1907 exposed was the absence of a lender of last resort. Walter Bagehot had described the solution in 1873: lend freely, at a penalty rate, against good collateral. But the United States had no institution capable of following this rule.

The National Banking System, created during the Civil War, required banks to hold reserves in other banks — creating chains of dependency. When interior banks needed cash, they withdrew from their New York correspondents. New York banks then contracted lending to shore up their own reserves. The crisis amplified through every link in the chain.

Trust companies operated with no reserve requirements and no regulatory oversight. They had captured market share by offering higher deposit rates and broader lending. When panic struck, they had nothing to draw on. Their collapse threatened to pull down the entire banking system.

The inelasticity of the currency supply made everything worse. Banks could not expand money supply in response to a liquidity crisis. Clearinghouses could issue certificates among member banks, but this was an ad hoc patch on a structural hole.

The deeper problem was political. American hostility to concentrated financial power — rooted in Jacksonian democracy — had killed two previous central banks. The country was ideologically opposed to the very institution it needed most.

J.P. Morgan convened the presidents of the major trust companies in his private library. He locked the doors. He then spent the night of November 2nd engineering a solution: the trusts would pool their resources to support one another. Morgan identified which trusts were solvent and which were not, and forced the solvent ones to contribute to a rescue fund.

Morgan also coordinated a rescue of the New York Stock Exchange, which was about to close for lack of funds on October 24th. He gathered twenty-five million dollars from the major banks in fifteen minutes and dispatched it to the exchange floor in time to prevent collapse.

Secretary of the Treasury George Cortelyou deposited twenty-five million dollars of government funds in New York banks, providing additional liquidity. This was improvisation — there was no legal framework for systematic intervention.

The crisis was contained, but the lesson was clear to Congress. No modern economy could depend on the personal authority and fortune of a single banker. The Aldrich-Vreeland Act of 1908 authorized emergency currency issuance and created the National Monetary Commission to study central banking.
The commission's work, and the secret Jekyll Island conference of 1910, produced the blueprint for the Federal Reserve System. The Federal Reserve Act passed in December 1913.

The Panic of 1907 lasted six weeks. The American economy contracted sharply in 1907 and 1908, with unemployment rising to eight percent. But recovery came relatively quickly, aided by the limited scope of bank failures compared to later crises.

The lasting result was institutional: the Federal Reserve System, established six years later, was the direct product of the 1907 crisis. The United States finally had a central bank capable of acting as lender of last resort.

The crisis also demonstrated the systemic risk posed by lightly regulated shadow banks. Trust companies had grown by exploiting regulatory gaps. The Panic revealed that deposit-taking institutions outside the regulated banking system posed risks to the entire financial system — a lesson that would need to be relearned in 2008.

J.P. Morgan died in 1913, the year the Federal Reserve opened. The institution he had made necessary through his indispensability replaced him. One man's authority had substituted for institutional design for too long. The modern central bank was, in part, his memorial.

The Panic of 1907 was, in one sense, the American counterpart to the Long Depression of 1873 (Episode 2): both crises exposed the absence of stabilising institutions that modern economies require. The 1907 crisis exposed it more dramatically, because it had a face — Morgan's — and a resolution that demanded an institutional replacement.
